title: Pastor Who Preached Divine Healing Over Medicine Got Arrested for Spreading a Pandemic
teaser: Rodney Howard-Browne, founder of The River at Tampa Bay Church, was arrested in March 2020 after holding packed services in defiance of a county stay-at-home order. The pastor who taught that God's healing trumps medical authority had quietly required his own staff to sign legal liability waivers first.
body:
There is a particular kind of theological confidence that expires the moment a sheriff's deputy shows up with handcuffs. For Rodney Howard-Browne, that moment arrived on March 30, 2020, when Hillsborough County authorities arrested him on charges of unlawful assembly and violation of a public health emergency order. The charges stemmed from two services held the previous day, March 29, 2020, at his Tampa megachurch, The River at Tampa Bay Church. By that date, Hillsborough County's stay-at-home order was already in effect, and the coronavirus pandemic had been declared a national emergency weeks earlier.

Howard-Browne is a South African-born minister who built his theological brand on the proposition that divine healing is not merely available but actively superior to medical authority. This is a common posture within certain charismatic and Word of Faith traditions, where sickness is framed as a failure of faith and medicine as a lesser substitute for God's provision. It is the kind of theology that preaches extremely well from a pulpit and tends to become considerably more complicated in the presence of a highly contagious respiratory virus with a documented fatality rate.

What made Howard-Browne's position structurally elegant, if spiritually awkward, was the simultaneous existence of legal liability waivers that church staff were required to sign. The waivers addressed legal exposure in the event of illness or harm related to attending church services. One does not typically construct a liability shield against consequences one believes to be impossible, which raises questions about exactly how absolute the pastor's faith in divine protection actually was.

The services on March 29 were described as packed, a word that carries epidemiological weight in the context of an airborne virus spreading through a population with no immunity or approved treatment. Howard-Browne had publicly framed his decision to hold services as an act of religious defiance and spiritual courage. The Hillsborough County Sheriff's Office framed it somewhat differently, as unlawful assembly and a public health violation.

Howard-Browne's defenders argued, as religious liberty advocates frequently do during public health emergencies, that government orders targeting church gatherings constitute a form of unconstitutional discrimination against religious practice. This is not a frivolous legal argument in the abstract. The tension between First Amendment protections and emergency public health authority is genuinely contested terrain in American constitutional law.

However, the specific detail that distinguishes Howard-Browne's case from a straightforward religious liberty dispute is the liability waiver. A religious leader who sincerely believes that gathering in faith eliminates health risk does not need to indemnify his institution against health-related claims from his staff. The waivers represent a candid acknowledgment, in legal language, that the risk being assumed is real, measurable, and potentially compensable.

The arrest itself was not a prolonged standoff. Howard-Browne was booked and released, and his legal team moved quickly to contest the charges on constitutional grounds. The broader story of COVID-era prosecutions of religious leaders became complicated as courts, including ultimately the U.S. Supreme Court, revisited the standards for applying emergency orders to houses of worship.

None of that subsequent legal complexity, however, resolves the original contradiction. Howard-Browne did not hold packed services and simultaneously require liability waivers because he was confused about his own theology. He did both things deliberately, and the coexistence of those two choices reveals something important about institutional risk management operating quietly beneath the surface of public proclamations of faith.

It is worth noting, without editorializing excessively, that the population most exposed to potential infection at those March 29 services was not Howard-Browne's legal team but the congregation members and staff who attended. The liability waivers protected the church as an institution. They did not protect the people sitting in the pews who may have absorbed the pastor's public confidence about divine protection without access to the institutional risk calculus happening behind the scenes.

Accountability journalism in the religious space requires distinguishing between theological disagreement and documented institutional contradiction. This case is not primarily about whether Howard-Browne's theology is correct or whether his reading of scripture is defensible. It is about the gap between what a religious leader tells his congregation in public and what that same leader's institution quietly documents in legal paperwork.

The Hillsborough County charges placed Howard-Browne in a lineage of pastors who discovered during the pandemic that the legal system does not issue exemptions for faith-based risk assessment. The arrest generated significant national attention and became a reference point in subsequent debates about religious gatherings and pandemic public health policy. Howard-Browne, to his credit, did not quietly retreat; he contested the arrest publicly and continued to maintain that his actions were justified.

That consistency of public posture is noted. What remains harder to reconcile is the liability waiver, a document that exists because someone in the institution ran the numbers and concluded that the risk was real enough to warrant legal protection. Theology and tort law are not always incompatible, but when they are applied simultaneously to the same situation in directly contradictory directions, one of them is doing the actual work. In this case, the paperwork is doing the work the pulpit would prefer not to discuss.
subjectTitle: Founder and Senior Pastor, The River at Tampa Bay Church
